11.2. Geometric picture

Let’s try to motivate this definition and reconcile it with the classical definition of conditional expectation:

(76)

The setup is as follows:

  1. Measurable functions wrt σ-fields of form subspaces of . Denote as these subspaces.
  2. Expectation is an inner product on .

  3. Conditional expectation is an orthogonal projection. In particular, we define as satisfying

    (77)

    This is exactly an orthogonality projection: the residual is orthogonal to all functions in the subspace. By the result from L25, this is unique (a.s. / modulo null sets) and it suffices to just check for events .

Let , so that are in the form . Then the classical conditional expectation (Equation 76) is the value of for .

Remark. This isn’t the full story. Conditional expectation still exists for . The picture is a corollary of the Radon-Nikodym theorem.

11.3. Filtrations and Martingales

Definition 25. A family of -fields is defined to be a filtration if . We will consider or . A stochastic process is said to be adapted to a filtration if 6 for every .

Example 5 (Coin Toss Filtration). Let , i.e. the set of functions , and define as the σ-field generated by the first coin tosses:

(78)

the smallest σ-field that makes each map measurable, i.e. the generators are events in the form or . Then is a filtration.

In the above example, is the set of all events that depend only on toss 1. Let and . Then the σ-field generated must contain . Since , we have

(79)

For , the generators are . We can instead use the four cylinder sets which partition 7. The σ-field generated by a partition contains all unions of those sets. For example for we have possible events: “first toss = H” is and “both tosses same” = . In general has events. You cannot directly see this from the primitive generators.

Definition 26. A stochastic process adapted to a filtration is defined to be a martingale if

When equality is substituting with , the process is called a submartingale/supermartingale respectively.

For a discrete time process, it suffices to just check be the tower law.

11.5. Gambling and Supermartingales

Suppose you bet an amount each round, and represents your wealth if you bet one dollar each round. Then your wealth is . Using the double the bet strategy we can leave with one dollar. But if we restrict ourselves to finite play then it turns out beating the gambling system is impossible. First establish the folllowing result.

Theorem 32. Suppose is a supermartingale and is predictable. Then the sequence defined by and is also a supermartingale.
Definition 28 (Stopping Time). Given a filtration on a sample space , a random variable is called a stopping time, if the event , for every .

Examples of stopping times:

  • [TODO]
Theorem 33. Suppose is a supermartingale and is a stopping time which is a.s. bounded. Then . In other words, if there exists a bound on the number of rounds for betting, then the expected net gain is non-positive, provided that in each round the expected gain is non-positive.

Sketch. [TODO]

Theorem 34 (Optional Stopping Theorem). Suppose is a supermartingale that is uniformly bounded8. THat is a.s., for some . Suppose is a stopping time. Then . If, in addition is a martingale, then .

This let’s us see again that if we use the “double the bet” algorithm with a maximum limit, then we expect nonpositive profit.

11.6. Doob-Kolmogorov Inequality

We establish the analog of Markov/Chebyshev’s to martingales.

Theorem 35 (Doob-Kolmogorov inequality). Suppose is a nonnegative submartingale adapted to and . Then for every

(82)

Interpretation: We can bound the worst case deviation of a submartingale using its value at the end of the time interval.

Remark. More generally we can use powers of for .

(83)

Remark. There is a continuous analog to this.
